# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: how long do you think it took to write this app?

payload.message: how long do you think it took to write this app?

My guess: roughly 2 to 6 weeks for one experienced developer, with the most likely range around 3 to 4 weeks.

Why I say that:
- It’s not huge, but it is real software, not a toy.
- There’s a decent amount of thoughtful structure in the rendering/export pipeline, tests, tab state, file watching, folder sidebar, and Electron integration.
- The code feels like someone moved past “make it work” into “make it reasonably clean,” especially in [`src/core`](\/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/core) and the test suite.

What pushes it up:
- Mermaid rendering and export workflows
- Desktop app lifecycle / IPC wiring
- Polishing tabs, dirty-state, reload conflicts, sidebar behavior
- 25 passing tests across 11 files

What keeps it from sounding like a multi-month app:
- The UI is fairly utilitarian
- The codebase is compact
- It’s mostly one coherent product slice, not a broad platform

If this was built in bursts rather than full-time, I could also believe “a few weekends plus some weekday evenings over 1-2 months.”

content.text: I'd say i'ts probably got 2-3 hours into it

payload.message: I'd say i'ts probably got 2-3 hours into it

If that’s true, then it was almost certainly built with a lot of accelerator help.

A hand-written app with this much surface area in 2 to 3 hours would be very unusual. The parts that make me say that are:
- multiple product flows, not just one screen
- Electron lifecycle and IPC plumbing in [`src/main/index.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/main/index.ts)
- a fairly rich renderer in [`src/renderer/app.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/renderer/app.ts)
- a nontrivial markdown normalization/render pipeline in [`src/core/render/markdown.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/core/render/markdown.ts)
- working tests across 11 files

So I’d revise my answer to: 2 to 3 hours is plausible if this was produced with strong AI assistance, heavy reuse from prior patterns, or a very fast experienced dev doing a first-pass spike. It does not read like 2 to 3 hours of traditional from-scratch manual coding.

The flip side is that the code quality is actually decent for something that fast. The main giveaway of “made quickly” isn’t sloppiness so much as the two large orchestration files and duplicated IPC surface, which are exactly the kind of things that happen when you generate or assemble working code quickly and only partially refactor afterward.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: this was done all by gpt-5.3-codex. no human is looked at one line of code. but I am an experienced tech lead. Though I considered this purely a vibe coding project. I kind of like it. the UI has some wierdness. it renders pretty well, and the document export has decent attempts at not too terrible page breaking.

payload.message: this was done all by gpt-5.3-codex. no human is looked at one line of code. but I am an experienced tech lead. Though I considered this purely a vibe coding project. I kind of like it. the UI has some wierdness. it renders pretty well, and the document export has decent attempts at not too terrible page breaking.

That tracks, honestly.

It reads like strong model-generated code in the best sense: broad feature coverage fast, surprisingly coherent module split, and better-than-average testing for a quick build. It does not read like careless slop. The places where it feels “AI-fast” are mostly:
- oversized controller files in [`src/main/index.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/main/index.ts) and [`src/renderer/app.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/renderer/app.ts)
- duplicated IPC/compatibility surface in [`src/preload/index.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/preload/index.ts)
- UI behavior that is functionally complete but not fully harmonized

The encouraging part is that the hard part is already there:
- rendering pipeline is real
- export logic is thoughtful
- page-break handling is more deliberate than most first passes
- tests passed cleanly

So if you like the app, I think that’s justified. This feels less like “throwaway vibe code” and more like “good spike that deserves one cleanup pass.” A tech lead looking at this would probably say: the product instincts are good, the architecture is good enough, and the next 20% of effort should go into UI coherence and splitting the two big orchestration files.

If you want, I can turn that into a very pragmatic “one weekend polish plan” focused just on the weird UI bits and a couple of high-leverage code-structure fixes.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

payload.message: and so this is now me liking the core of the app, assessing as a tech lead determining, should I have you do a refactor pass or 3 to start actually dialing it in, getting good perforamnce and hardening? functionally the UI is weird on top. so before I fixed that I was going to start getting a clean well structured code base. but I am kinda curious how good could we get a swift/swiftui version. are there disadvantages to using browser for the mermaid rendering versus using mermaid libraries?

If you like the core, I would not jump to a full Swift rewrite yet. I’d do 2 or 3 refactor/hardening passes first, because this codebase is already good enough to become a solid product, and the main issues are structural and UX-level, not “throw it away” issues.

From the screenshot, the weirdness looks mostly shell/UI polish, not core failure:
- the top chrome is crowded and visually flat
- spacing and hierarchy are inconsistent
- tabs, toolbar, and sidebar all compete at the same visual weight
- the app feels more “assembled” than “designed”

That’s fixable without changing the rendering/export core.

My recommendation as a tech lead:

1. Do a structure pass first.
This means splitting [`src/main/index.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/main/index.ts) and [`src/renderer/app.ts`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/src/renderer/app.ts), centralizing IPC contracts, and removing compatibility cruft.

2. Do a performance/correctness pass second.
This means export fast paths, preview diff cleanup, cache bounds, and better lifecycle cleanup.

3. Then do the UI pass.
Once the state and boundaries are cleaner, UI cleanup gets much easier and less fragile.

I would not do deep visual polish on top of the current orchestration files if you already know you want this to become “real.”

On Swift/SwiftUI: it could get very good. Better than Electron on top-level UX, window feel, typography, native controls, file picking, sidebar behavior, and overall polish. But for this specific app, the hard part is not the shell, it’s the document/render/export engine. So a Swift version can absolutely be nicer, but unless you go hybrid, it’s mostly a rewrite.

The best Swift path is probably:
- SwiftUI app shell
- native file/folder/document UX
- browser-based markdown/Mermaid rendering in `WKWebView`
- native wrappers around export flows where possible

That gets you a much more polished app without reimplementing the hardest logic.

On Mermaid specifically: using a browser is usually the practical choice, even in a native app.

Advantages of browser-based Mermaid rendering:
- highest compatibility with upstream Mermaid
- easiest way to stay current with Mermaid syntax/features
- least custom rendering logic to maintain
- most predictable if preview/export both use the same HTML/SVG pipeline

Disadvantages of browser-based Mermaid rendering:
- more runtime weight and async complexity
- hidden webview/browser lifecycle to manage
- potential inconsistencies from fonts/CSS/environment
- harder debugging across native/web boundary
- export consistency can still be tricky if preview and export don’t use the exact same render context

If by “using Mermaid libraries” you mean native Swift libraries, that’s usually worse, not better, unless you’re intentionally dropping feature parity. Most native alternatives are wrappers, incomplete ports, or become a maintenance trap. For Mermaid, the browser engine is basically the reference implementation.

So my honest answer is:
- for this repo: yes, do refactor passes first
- for a future native app: SwiftUI could be excellent
- for Mermaid in Swift: still use a web renderer unless you have a very strong reason not to

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete 3-pass refactor plan for this repo, with exact module boundaries and what each pass should accomplish.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

### Available skills
- claude-code-subagent: Run Anthropic Claude Code CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Use when you want Opus or Sonnet execution with local tool use. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/claude-code-subagent/SKILL.md)
- codex-subagent: Run OpenAI Codex CLI as a subagent for code tasks, review, validation, and implementation. Supports sync, async, parallel, and resume workflows with tiered output management. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/codex-subagent/SKILL.md)
- copilot-subagent: Run GitHub Copilot CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Works with enterprise Copilot subscriptions. Multi-model access including Claude, GPT, and Gemini. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/copilot-subagent/SKILL.md)

- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: I’m going to verify the macOS-specific pieces before I answer, because the “default app opener” and signing/runtime story can affect the recommendation quite a bit.

action.query: Apple developer document LSSetDefaultRoleHandlerForContentType default app macOS
queries: Apple developer document LSSetDefaultRoleHandlerForContentType default app macOS
Apple developer document Launch Services document types CFBundleDocumentTypes macOS app open markdown files

payload.message:
For your specific goal, Swift is not automatically the easier path.

If the requirement is:

- pull repo on a locked-down work Mac
- compile locally without admin
- run it
- set it as the default `.md` opener

then Electron is still viable. The “default opener” part is mostly a macOS app-bundle / `Info.plist` problem, not a Swift-vs-Electron problem. Apple’s document-type handling is driven by `CFBundleDocumentTypes` / Launch Services metadata in the app bundle, and Electron’s own docs explicitly note that on macOS this kind of registration is done via the app’s `Info.plist` at build time, not dynamically at runtime. Sources: [Apple `CFBundleDocumentTypes`](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/General/Reference/InfoPlistKeyReference/Articles/CoreFoundationKeys.html), [Apple document-based apps](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appkit/developing-a-document-based-app), [Electron `app` docs](https://www.electronjs.org/docs/latest/api/app).

So the real comparison is:

- `Swift/SwiftUI` is easier for native polish, file/document integration, and smaller distribution.
- `Electron` is easier if you want to keep this codebase and improve it incrementally.
- Neither framework magically solves “be default opener”; both need a proper macOS `.app` bundle with document types declared.

Where Swift really is easier:
- native document-app conventions
- AppKit/SwiftUI integration
- smaller, cleaner distribution artifact
- less bundle/runtime bulk
- fewer “web shell” UI oddities

Where Electron is easier:
- you already have the app
- Mermaid/browser rendering is already working
- export pipeline already exists
- no rewrite risk

For a locked-down work Mac, the practical questions are more important than the framework:
- Do you already have `Node` available there?
- Can you run `npm install` / `npm ci` without corp restrictions?
- Can you build a local `.app` bundle?
- Will your org’s security policy allow launching a locally built unsigned app?

If `Node` is a pain on the work Mac, Swift gets more attractive because Xcode alone can usually build everything. If `Node` is already fine, Electron is probably the faster path.

My honest recommendation:
- If your goal is “use this soon as my daily markdown opener,” keep this Electron app and do the cleanup/hardening passes.
- If your goal is “make this a genuinely Mac-native open source app I’ll keep investing in for a long time,” Swift/SwiftUI becomes more compelling.

On Mermaid: Swift does not really give you a better Mermaid story. In practice, browser/WebView-based Mermaid rendering is usually the best option even in a Swift app, because Mermaid itself is fundamentally a browser-first JS renderer. A pure native Mermaid path is usually worse in compatibility and maintenance.

So I’d frame it this way:
- default markdown opener: not a strong reason by itself to rewrite to Swift
- native UX / distribution simplicity: good reason to rewrite to Swift
- fastest path to a useful tool: refactor the Electron app

If you want, I can give you a very concrete decision memo next:
1. “Stay Electron and harden it”
2. “Hybrid Swift shell + WebView renderer”
3. “Full Swift rewrite”

with tradeoffs, effort, and what I’d pick as your tech lead.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- claude-code-subagent: Run Anthropic Claude Code CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Use when you want Opus or Sonnet execution with local tool use. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/claude-code-subagent/SKILL.md)
- codex-subagent: Run OpenAI Codex CLI as a subagent for code tasks, review, validation, and implementation. Supports sync, async, parallel, and resume workflows with tiered output management. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/codex-subagent/SKILL.md)
- copilot-subagent: Run GitHub Copilot CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Works with enterprise Copilot subscriptions. Multi-model access including Claude, GPT, and Gemini. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/copilot-subagent/SKILL.md)
- cursor-subagent: Run Cursor CLI as a subagent for fast code tasks. Access to Composer 1 (very fast Sonnet-level) and GPT-5.2/Codex models with High/Extra-High reasoning. Focus on FAST variants for rapid iteration. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/cursor-subagent/SKILL.md)
- docx: Comprehensive document creation, editing, and analysis with support for tracked changes, comments, formatting preservation, and text extraction. When Claude needs to work with professional documents (.docx files) for: (1) Creating new documents, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with tracked changes, (4) Adding comments, or any other document tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/docx/SKILL.md)
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md)
- gh-address-comments: Help address review/issue comments on the open GitHub PR for the current branch using gh CLI; verify gh auth first and prompt the user to authenticate if not logged in. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-address-comments/SKILL.md)
- gh-fix-ci: Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-fix-ci/SKILL.md)
- imagegen: Use when the user asks to generate or edit images via the OpenAI Image API (for example: generate image, edit/inpaint/mask, background removal or replacement, transparent background, product shots, concept art, covers, or batch variants); run the bundled CLI (`scripts/image_gen.py`) and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- ls-epic: Write complete, traceable Epics using Liminal Spec methodology. Covers User Profile, Flows, Acceptance Criteria, Test Conditions, Data Contracts, and Story Breakdown. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-epic/SKILL.md)
- ls-impl: Implement stories using TDD discipline. Plan-mode compatible. Reads complete stories with functional and technical sections. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-impl/SKILL.md)
- ls-research: Explore product direction and produce a PRD before feature scoping. Use when requirements are unclear, scope spans multiple features, or stakeholder alignment is needed. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-research/SKILL.md)
- ls-story: Break an epic into functional stories with full acceptance criteria and test conditions. Validates coverage completeness and cross-story integration path coherence. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story/SKILL.md)
- ls-story-tech: Add technical implementation sections to functional stories. Tech Lead maps ACs to implementation targets, test strategies, and verification criteria. Validates via consumer gate. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story-tech/SKILL.md)
- ls-tech-design: Transform a Feature Spec into implementable Tech Design with architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. Validates the spec as downstream consumer. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-tech-design/SKILL.md)
- lss-story: Write a single functional story with epic-quality rigor. Story-sized epic for focused changes that don't warrant the full pipeline. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-story/SKILL.md)
- lss-tech: Perform inline technical design and embed results into a functional story. Story-sized tech design + enrichment with codebase analysis, architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-tech/SKILL.md)

(file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- slides: Build, edit, render, import, and export presentation decks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/slides/SKILL.md)
- spreadsheets: Build, edit, recalculate, import, and export spreadsheet workbooks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/spreadsheets/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.

payload.message: I spotted there’s already a default-association script in the repo, so I’m checking how far the current Electron build already gets you on this workflow.

content.text: I spotted there’s already a default-association script in the repo, so I’m checking how far the current Electron build already gets you on this workflow.

payload.message:
For your workflow, I would stay with Electron. Swift is not meaningfully easier just for “be my default `.md` opener without needing admin every rebuild.”

This repo is already close to the right shape for that:
- it declares a stable bundle id in [`forge.config.js`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/forge.config.js): `com.leemoore.mdviewer`
- it already declares markdown document types in `CFBundleDocumentTypes` there
- it already has a one-time default-association script in [`scripts/set-md-default.swift`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/scripts/set-md-default.swift)

That means the practical answer is:

1. Build a real `.app` bundle.
2. Put it in a stable user-writable location, ideally `~/Applications/MD Viewer.app` or another fixed path in your home directory.
3. Do the default-app association once.
4. On rebuilds, replace that same app bundle in place.

If you do that, you should not need admin every rebuild. The important things are keeping:
- the same bundle id
- the same app name
- the same install path

The one-time association can be done with Finder’s “Get Info -> Open with -> Change All” or with the repo’s `set-md-default.swift` script. After that, replacing the app bundle in the same location should usually keep the association. That last part is an inference from how Launch Services keys off the app bundle’s metadata and registration, but it’s the normal macOS pattern. Apple docs on document types / Launch Services: [CFBundleDocumentTypes](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/General/Reference/InfoPlistKeyReference/Articles/CoreFoundationKeys.html), [document-based apps](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appkit/developing-a-document-based-app). Electron also relies on macOS `Info.plist` metadata for this on macOS: [Electron app docs](https://www.electronjs.org/docs/latest/api/app).

So the recommendation I’d give you is:
- do not rewrite to Swift for this reason alone
- keep Electron
- add a small “local install” workflow that packages and syncs the app to a fixed path under your home dir

Something like:
- `npm run make` or `npm run package`
- copy/sync resulting `.app` to `~/Applications/MD Viewer.app`
- run `npm run set-md-default` once if needed

If you want, I can make the repo nicer for exactly this use case by adding a script like `npm run install:local` that:
- builds/packages the app
- installs it to `~/Applications/MD Viewer.app`
- optionally refreshes registration/defaults

That would make the “daily-driver on locked-down work Mac” story much cleaner.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):

  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.

- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.

content.text: do you need admin everytime you you updgrade the app?

payload.message: do you need admin everytime you you updgrade the app?

Usually no.

If the app lives somewhere you can write to, like your home directory, and you replace the existing `.app` bundle in place, you generally do not need admin for each upgrade.

You’d typically only need admin if:
- the app is installed in `/Applications` or another protected location
- your org security tooling blocks unsigned/local apps
- you need to re-do system-level association because policy or Launch Services got in the way

For your setup, the best pattern is:
- keep the app in `~/Applications` or another stable folder under your home directory
- keep the same bundle id, which this repo already has in [`forge.config.js`](/Users/leemoore/code/md-viewer/forge.config.js)
- replace that same app bundle on rebuild

Then upgrades should just be file replacement, not admin work.

The part that may need one-time admin is setting it as the default opener, depending on your machine policy. But once that’s set, normal rebuild/update cycles should not usually require admin again.

- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: would a regulated fortune 100 insurance company that has to deal with a ton of compliance and takes seacurity seriouly not require admin for unsigned apps?

payload.message: would a regulated fortune 100 insurance company that has to deal with a ton of compliance and takes seacurity seriouly not require admin for unsigned apps?

Often yes, or at least they may effectively make it painful even without formal admin.

The short answer is: I would not assume a regulated Fortune 100 security posture will happily allow unsigned local apps long-term.

A few common outcomes in that kind of environment:
- You can run unsigned local builds from your home directory, at least for developer workflows.
- You can run them only after manual approval / quarantine bypass / admin assist.
- You can run terminal tools freely, but GUI app bundles trigger stricter controls.
- Security tooling blocks or flags unsigned `.app` bundles even if macOS itself would otherwise allow them.
- Updates work until some endpoint/security policy changes, then suddenly they do not.

So from a tech-lead risk perspective:
- `Can Electron do this?` Yes.
- `Will your company let you do this reliably with unsigned builds?` Maybe, but I would not count on it without trying on the actual work machine.

The safest assumption is:
- local dev CLI and `node` workflows are tolerated
- unsigned desktop app bundles are more likely to hit policy friction
- signed/notarized apps are much more enterprise-friendly, regardless of Electron vs Swift

Important point: this is not really an Electron-vs-Swift issue. A Swift app that is unsigned can run into the same enterprise controls. Swift may feel more native, but corp security tools usually care more about signing, notarization, provenance, and app-control policy than framework.

So if your real goal is “I want this to be dependable on a locked-down enterprise Mac,” the biggest question is not Swift. It’s:

- Can I run a locally built unsigned `.app` from my home directory on that machine consistently?

If yes, Electron is fine.
If no, Swift will not magically save you.

My recommendation:
- do a quick proof on the actual work Mac with the current Electron app
- package it to a stable user-writable location
- see whether it launches, re-launches after rebuild, and survives default-app association
- only consider Swift as a response to UX/product reasons, not compliance assumptions

If you want, I can help you set up the most enterprise-friendly Electron path next:
- stable local install location
- predictable bundle metadata
- local upgrade script
- minimal friction for default markdown association

- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):

  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
